_readme: COURSE MANIFEST — KIR-EMC-001 Electromagnetic Compatibility for AC Drive Systems.
Source material: Danfoss Motor and Drive Text, Chapter 6 — Electromagnetic Compatibility.
Run _build_course.ps1 from the workspace root to generate wrapper HTML files.
JSON does not support comments. Fields prefixed with '_comment' are documentation only.

course.title: Electromagnetic Compatibility for AC Drive Systems

intro.subtitle: EMC Principles and Practice for AC Drive Systems

screens.label: Title Card
vo: Welcome to this course on electromagnetic compatibility for AC drive systems. We'll cover why AC drives are potential sources of interference, how to control that interference through correct installation, and how to meet the compliance requirements of the EMC Directive. Use the Play button to start the voiceover on each screen. Navigate forward and back with the arrow buttons. You must pass each section quiz before moving on. Let's get started.

screens.label: Who This Course Is For
vo: This course is for engineers, technicians, and technical sales staff who specify, design, install, or commission AC drive systems. If you've ever dealt with interference problems on a drive installation, or needed to select the right motor cable, filter, or earthing strategy, this course is for you. It's also valuable for anyone who needs to understand CE marking requirements and what the EMC Directive demands of a drive installation.

screens.label: Course Application
vo: Throughout this course, our reference context is a typical industrial AC drive installation — a variable speed drive controlling an induction motor in a factory environment. The installation must comply with the European EMC Directive, avoid interference with other equipment on the mains supply, protect sensitive control signals from noise, and avoid premature bearing failures in the motor. Every principle we cover will be grounded in that practical scenario.

screens.label: Learning Objectives
vo: By the end of this course, you will be able to achieve the following five learning objectives.
One. Explain the concepts of EMI and EMC, and identify the four interference coupling mechanisms.
Two. Apply correct grounding and shielding techniques, including proper cable shield termination.
Three. Select appropriate output filters — du/dt, sine-wave, or common-mode — based on application requirements.
Four. Identify the causes of electrical bearing stress and apply the relevant mitigation measures.
Five. Classify an AC drive installation against the EN/IEC 61800-3 C1 to C4 categories and identify the applicable EMC standards.
These five objectives cover everything you need to make sound EMC decisions on a real drive installation.

objectives.text: Explain the concepts of EMI and EMC, and identify the four interference coupling mechanisms.
Apply correct grounding and shielding techniques, including proper 360° shield termination and ground-loop prevention.
Select appropriate output filters (du/dt, sine-wave, common-mode) based on application requirements.
Identify the causes of electrical bearing stress (EDM) and apply the relevant mechanical and electrical mitigation measures.
Identify the EMC Directive scope and classify an AC drive installation against the EN/IEC 61800-3 C1–C4 categories.

int2_cards.body: This course supports engineers, technicians, and technical sales staff involved with AC drive specification, installation, and commissioning.
Applications focus on industrial AC drive installations where EMC affects system performance, regulatory compliance, and equipment life.
A solid understanding of EMC enables correct installation decisions, faster fault diagnosis, and confident compliance with CE marking requirements.

int3_kp.body:
Reference scenario:
A variable speed AC drive controlling an induction motor in an industrial plant — requiring CE marking compliance and interference-free operation.

Source material:
Danfoss Motor and Drive Text, Chapter 6 — Electromagnetic Compatibility.
Principles apply across all major drive manufacturers.

intro.int3_highlight: ⚡ AC drives are one of the most common sources of EMI in industrial installations.

int3_closing.body: Every installation decision — cable type, grounding strategy, filter selection — either manages or worsens the EMC behaviour of the system.

screens.label: Four Coupling Mechanisms
screens.vo: Section one: What is EMC. Before we can control interference, we need to understand how it travels. There are four coupling mechanisms. Galvanic coupling — two circuits share a conductive connection. Capacitive coupling — noise transfers between conductors through parasitic capacitances. Inductive coupling — a current-carrying conductor induces voltage in a nearby conductor. And electromagnetic coupling — energy radiates from a transmitting conductor and is picked up by a receiving conductor. Every EMI problem has a source, a coupling path, and a victim. [PAUSE] Identifying which coupling mechanism is active is the first step in fixing the problem.

screens.label: EMI vs. EMC
screens.vo: Electromagnetic interference is the degradation of equipment performance caused by electromagnetic disturbance. Electromagnetic compatibility — or EMC — is the ability of equipment to operate correctly in its electromagnetic environment without itself causing unacceptable disturbance to other equipment. EMC has two sides. Emission: the unintended electromagnetic output of a device must be limited. And immunity: the device must tolerate a reasonable level of interference from its environment. The gap between the emission level and the immunity threshold is called the compatibility gap. The wider that gap, the more margin you have. AC drives must be both controlled in emission and adequately immune — and the installation you design affects both.

screens.label: Common-Mode and Differential-Mode
screens.vo: When we talk about conducted interference, two terms come up constantly: common-mode and differential-mode. Differential-mode noise travels on both conductors of a circuit in opposite directions — it's in series with the useful signal. Common-mode noise travels on both conductors in the same direction, with its return path through the reference ground. [PAUSE] You'll also hear the term RFI — radio frequency interference. RFI is an older term referring specifically to interference with radio signals. EMC is the broader, modern term covering interference with any electrical equipment. For our purposes, you can treat them as interchangeable in most conversations.

screens.label: Why AC Drives Generate EMI
screens.vo: AC drives switch voltages at rates measured in thousands of volts per microsecond. This high rate of change — the du/dt — is applied across parasitic capacitances to ground in the motor cable and motor windings. The result is a common-mode current that must return to its source in the DC link. [PAUSE] Inside the drive, common-mode capacitors — one in the RFI filter circuit, one in the DC link — provide a local return path. When a shielded motor cable is correctly connected at both ends, most of that current returns through the cable shield and the drive chassis. Without a shielded cable, the current returns through the mains supply, causing interference in other connected equipment.

quiz.quiz_vo: Before moving on to Section two, let's check your understanding of the EMC fundamentals we've just covered. You'll be presented with four questions drawn from a pool. You need three correct answers to proceed.
quiz.pass_msg: Well done — you have a solid grasp of EMC fundamentals. Proceed to Section 2.
quiz.fail_msg: Review the section and try again. Focus on the coupling mechanisms and the role of common-mode current.

pool.stem: A noise source and its victim circuit share a direct conductive connection. Which coupling mechanism is this?

options.text: Capacitive coupling
Galvanic coupling
Inductive coupling
Electromagnetic coupling

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Galvanic coupling occurs when two circuits share a common conductive connection, allowing noise to travel directly between them.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Galvanic coupling is the mechanism where noise travels via a shared conductive path between source and victim.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — think about which mechanism involves a direct electrical connection between source and victim.

pool.stem: Which of the following best describes electromagnetic compatibility (EMC)?

options.text: The ability of equipment to emit no electromagnetic interference at all
A legal requirement to obtain CE marking in the EU
The ability of equipment to function in its electromagnetic environment without causing unacceptable disturbance to other equipment
A measure of how well an RFI filter suppresses high-frequency noise

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. EMC encompasses both immunity (functioning in the presence of interference) and emission (not causing unacceptable disturbance to others).
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. EMC is defined as the ability to function satisfactorily in the electromagnetic environment without introducing intolerable disturbances. It covers both emission and immunity.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — consider that EMC has two sides: how much a device emits, and how well it tolerates interference.

pool.stem: Common-mode noise in a cable is characterised by:

options.text: Noise travelling on both conductors in opposite directions
Noise travelling on both conductors in the same direction, returning via the reference ground
Noise that only appears on the shielded conductor
Noise caused by magnetic coupling from an adjacent power cable

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Common-mode noise travels on both conductors in the same direction and returns through the common reference ground.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Common-mode noise uses both conductors as the forward path and the reference ground as the return path. Differential-mode travels in opposite directions on each conductor.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — consider how the currents on each conductor compare in common-mode versus differential-mode.

pool.stem: In an AC drive installation, what is the primary source of common-mode current?

options.text: The mains supply transformer
The DC-link capacitors discharging into the load
The high du/dt switching voltage applied across parasitic capacitances to ground in the motor cable and motor
The motor's back-EMF during regenerative braking

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. The inverter's high du/dt voltage is applied across parasitic capacitances to ground, driving a common-mode current proportional to C × du/dt.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The common-mode current is generated by the steep voltage switching (high du/dt) of the inverter acting on parasitic capacitances between the conductors and ground.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — think about what the inverter does to the output voltage and what that means for capacitively coupled paths to ground.

pool.stem: Why is a 4–20 mA current reference preferred over a 0–10 V voltage reference in an AC drive installation?

options.text: Current signals are cheaper to wire
Current signals are less susceptible to common-mode noise than voltage signals
Voltage signals require a separate power supply
The 0–10 V range is outside the drive's input specification

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Current signals are less susceptible to noise than voltage signals, making 4–20 mA the preferred choice when analogue references cannot be avoided.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The advantage of a current loop signal is its greater immunity to electromagnetic noise compared to a voltage reference.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — consider which signal type is inherently less affected by voltage noise induced on the signal cable.

pool.stem: What is the purpose of the common-mode capacitors inside an AC drive?

options.text: To smooth the DC-link voltage ripple at power frequency
To provide a local return path for high-frequency common-mode currents
To block differential-mode noise from reaching the motor
To limit the peak output voltage of the inverter

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Common-mode capacitors (in the RFI filter and DC link) give high-frequency common-mode currents a low-impedance return path to the DC link, keeping them out of the mains supply.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The common-mode capacitors provide a local HF return path so that common-mode currents circulate within the drive and cable rather than returning through the mains.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — think about where the common-mode current needs to return to, and what component helps it do so locally.

pool.stem: What is the 'compatibility gap' in the context of EMC?

options.text: The physical clearance required between power and signal cables
The frequency band not covered by the EMC standard
The difference between the emission level and the immunity threshold
The margin between the rated voltage and the breakdown voltage of the cable insulation

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. The compatibility gap is the margin between the emission level of a source and the immunity threshold of the victim. A wider gap means more EMC margin.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The compatibility gap describes the difference between how much interference a device emits and how much another device can tolerate.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — think about the relationship between emission margin and immunity margin on the EMC level diagram.

screens.label: Why Grounding Matters
screens.vo: Section two: Grounding and Shielding. Grounding means connecting electrical equipment to a common reference point. There are two distinct reasons for doing this. Safety grounding ensures that if electrical insulation degrades, no live voltage appears on touchable surfaces — protecting people from electric shock. Signal grounding reduces voltage potential differences between pieces of equipment, which prevents noise coupling between them. [PAUSE] It is critical to remember that safety always takes priority over EMC. If a grounding arrangement is required for safety, it cannot be modified for EMC purposes — even if that modification would reduce interference. Safety first, always.

screens.label: Grounding Configurations
screens.vo: There are three common grounding configurations. Single-point series grounding connects equipment one after another to a single ground point — simple, but the impedance increases down the chain. Single-point parallel grounding connects each piece of equipment directly to the ground point — better impedance, but uses more conductors. Multi-point grounding connects equipment to ground at multiple local points, minimising the length of each ground connection and therefore minimising impedance. [PAUSE] In AC drive installations, what matters most is that the impedance of the grounding connection is as low as possible — especially at high frequencies — to equalise potentials across the connected equipment.

screens.label: Cable Shielding Performance
screens.vo: Not all shielded cables provide the same level of protection. The shielding performance of a cable is measured by its transfer impedance — the voltage generated on the inside of the shield by a current on the outside. Lower transfer impedance means better shielding. [PAUSE] As you can see on screen, cable types range from aluminium foil with a drain wire — the least effective — up through single-layer braided copper wire, dual-layer braid, and finally conductors in rigid steel or copper conduit. For most AC drive motor cable applications, single-layer braided copper wire is the practical choice: it offers good shielding performance at a reasonable cost.

screens.label: Correct Shield Termination
screens.vo: How you terminate the cable shield is just as important as the cable type. The shield must be connected to the equipment chassis through a full 360-degree clamp connection — never through a pigtail. A pigtail dramatically increases transfer impedance and destroys the shielding effect. Terminate both ends of the motor cable shield. Only terminating one end significantly reduces the shield's effectiveness. [PAUSE] Signal cables sometimes suffer from ground loops — a mains-frequency current circulating through the shield because two pieces of equipment are at different ground potentials. In that case, connect one end of the shield via a 100 nanofarad capacitor. This breaks the ground loop at 50 hertz while keeping the high-frequency shield connection intact.

quiz.quiz_vo: Let's check your understanding of grounding and shielding. Four questions from the pool — you need three correct to proceed.
quiz.pass_msg: Excellent — you understand the key principles of grounding and shielding. Move on to Section 3.
quiz.fail_msg: Review the section — particularly shield termination requirements and ground loop mitigation — then try again.

pool.stem: In an AC drive installation, what takes the highest priority when making grounding decisions?

options.text: Minimising high-frequency impedance for EMC
Compliance with the EMC Directive
Electrical safety
Reducing mains-frequency ground currents

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Electrical safety always has the highest priority. EMC grounding must never compromise the safety grounding arrangement.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Electrical safety always comes first — no EMC requirement overrides it.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — consider what happens if the insulation of a live conductor degrades and there is no safety ground.

pool.stem: What does transfer impedance measure in a shielded cable?

options.text: The resistance of the shield conductor at 50 Hz
The voltage generated inside the shield by a current flowing on the outside of the shield
The capacitance between the shield and the inner conductors
The insertion loss of the shield at the cable's operating frequency

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Transfer impedance relates the current on the outer surface of the shield to the voltage it generates on the inner surface. Lower ZT means better shielding.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Transfer impedance is defined as the voltage generated on the inside of the shield per unit length per unit current on the outside — it quantifies how effectively the shield isolates internal from external fields.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — think about what it means for a shield to be effective at blocking external interference from reaching the inner conductors.

pool.stem: A motor cable shield is connected to the drive chassis using a short length of wire twisted into a bundle (a pigtail). What is the consequence?

options.text: No effect — the shield is still connected to ground
The transfer impedance is dramatically increased, destroying the shielding effectiveness at high frequency
The pigtail improves contact resistance at the termination point
Ground loop currents are prevented

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. A pigtail termination dramatically increases transfer impedance, especially at high frequencies, and largely defeats the purpose of using a shielded cable.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. While the shield is technically connected, a pigtail introduces inductance that increases transfer impedance at high frequency — ruining the shield's effectiveness.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — consider that the inductance of even a short pigtail matters greatly at the high frequencies produced by the drive's switching.

pool.stem: How many ends of a motor cable shield should be connected to chassis ground?

options.text: Only the drive end, to avoid ground loops
Only the motor end, as the drive has internal common-mode capacitors
Both ends, with a 360-degree clamp at each end
Neither end — the shield must float to avoid saturating the RFI filter

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Both ends of the motor cable shield must be connected with a 360-degree clamp. Terminating only one end significantly reduces shielding effectiveness and can cause interference problems.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Both ends must be terminated with a 360-degree connection. Single-end termination is only sometimes used for low-frequency signal cables where a ground loop exists — never for motor cables.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — recall the guidance on motor cable shield termination and what happens when only one end is connected.

pool.stem: A 50 Hz ground current is detected in the shield of a serial communication cable between a drive and a PLC. What is the recommended solution?

options.text: Connect the shield at both ends using pigtails
Remove the shield connection at both ends
First try a potential-equalising cable; if not possible, connect one shield end through a 100 nF capacitor
Replace the shielded cable with an unshielded twisted pair

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. A potential-equalising cable is the best solution. If not possible, a 100 nF capacitor on one shield end breaks the 50 Hz ground loop while maintaining the HF shield connection.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The 100 nF capacitor solution is designed for this situation: it presents high impedance at 50 Hz (blocking the loop current) but low impedance at high frequency (maintaining shield effectiveness).
feedback.retry: Incorrect — consider a solution that breaks the low-frequency loop while keeping the high-frequency shield connection intact.

pool.stem: Which cable type offers the best shielding performance for an AC drive motor cable?

options.text: Aluminium foil with a copper drain wire
Single-layer braided copper wire
Dual-layer braided copper wire with a high-permeability middle layer
Twisted copper wire armoured cable

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Dual-layer braided copper wire with a high-permeability middle layer offers the best shielding performance, though single-layer braid is the most common practical choice due to cost.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Dual-layer braid with a high-permeability middle layer has the lowest transfer impedance. Single-layer braid is the most common choice but does not match the performance of dual-layer construction.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — review the cable shielding performance ranking from poorest to best.

pool.stem: Multi-point grounding is preferred in AC drive installations primarily because:

options.text: It is simpler to design and wire
It reduces the length of each ground connection, minimising high-frequency impedance
It eliminates the need for a shielded motor cable
It prevents common-mode currents from forming in the motor cable

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Multi-point grounding keeps each ground connection short, which minimises its impedance at high frequency — the key requirement for effective EMC grounding.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The advantage of multi-point grounding is the short length of each connection, which translates directly to lower impedance at the high frequencies present in drive systems.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — consider what determines the effectiveness of a ground connection at the high switching frequencies of an AC drive.

screens.label: EMC Installation Plan
screens.vo: Section three: EMC-Correct Installation and Output Filters. Good EMC doesn't happen by accident — it requires planning. When designing an AC drive installation, work through this seven-step EMC plan. First, list all components and areas. Second, classify them as potential noise sources or potentially sensitive equipment. Third, classify the cables connecting them as noisy or sensitive. Fourth, set requirements and select equipment accordingly. Fifth, physically separate noise sources from sensitive equipment. Sixth, control the interfaces between them. And seventh, route cables according to their classification. [PAUSE] Separating, shielding, and routing — these three verbs summarise the entire plan.

screens.label: Practical Installation Measures
screens.vo: The diagram shows a typical AC drive cabinet installation. Let's walk through the key measures. The drive must make direct metal-to-metal contact with the backplate — establishing a low-impedance high-frequency ground. Shielded motor and brake cables must be used, with the shield terminated in a 360-degree EMC cable gland at the drive. At the motor terminal box, the shield must also be clamped 360 degrees. Control cables are segregated and routed away from power cables. A potential equalisation conductor of at least 10 square millimetres connects the drive and motor chassis. [PAUSE] Skipping any one of these measures can introduce interference that compromises the entire installation.

screens.label: When to Use Output Filters
screens.vo: Variable speed drives produce several unwanted secondary effects. Motor winding insulation stress from high du/dt voltages. Bearing electrical stress from common-mode currents. Acoustic switching noise. And electromagnetic interference on the supply. In most applications these effects are at acceptable levels and no output filters are needed. But in some cases — old motors, long cables, 690 volt supply, or high-power applications — filters are needed. [PAUSE] There are three main filter types: du/dt filters, sine-wave filters, and common-mode filters. Each addresses a different subset of these effects, at different cost and with different trade-offs.

screens.label: du/dt vs. Sine-Wave Filters
screens.vo: Du/dt filters are LC filters tuned above the switching frequency. They reduce the slew rate of the inverter's output voltage to below 500 volts per microsecond. This protects motor winding insulation. The voltage waveform remains pulse-width modulated — just with slower edges. Du/dt filters are recommended with old motors with poor insulation, with 690 volt supply systems, and in short-cable applications. They are not effective with long cables, because the cable itself reduces du/dt at the motor terminals anyway. [PAUSE] Sine-wave filters go further: they produce a near-perfect sinusoidal output voltage, eliminating voltage reflections, bearing currents, and acoustic noise. They allow much longer motor cables. But they are significantly more expensive and cause a 7 to 10 percent voltage drop.

screens.label: Common-Mode Filters
screens.vo: Common-mode filters use toroidal cores made of nanocrystalline material to increase the impedance of common-mode currents. The three motor phases are passed through the cores. Because differential-mode currents sum to zero, they produce no net magnetic flux and are unaffected by the cores. Common-mode currents produce a net flux, which is concentrated in the ferrite material — dramatically increasing impedance for common-mode currents only. [PAUSE] The cores can be mounted at the drive output terminals or inside the motor terminal box. Drive-terminal mounting reduces noise on the cable but risks core saturation in long-cable applications. Motor-terminal-box mounting avoids saturation but leaves the cable radiating. The PE conductor must never pass through the cores — and the filter should be installed directly at the drive output, before any other output filters.

quiz.quiz_vo: Time to check your understanding of installation practices and filter selection. Four questions from the pool — three correct needed to proceed.
quiz.pass_msg: Well done — you can apply EMC installation principles and select appropriate output filters. Proceed to Section 4.
quiz.fail_msg: Review the section — particularly the filter comparison and the practical installation measures — then try again.

pool.stem: An AC drive is installed on a painted backplate. The drive's mounting feet are bolted through the paint. What is the EMC problem?

options.text: The paint acts as insulation, increasing the impedance of the high-frequency ground connection
The drive will not meet safety requirements without a separate PE conductor
Bolt connections are not permitted in EMC-sensitive installations
The backplate cannot carry the fault current in case of a short circuit

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Paint is an insulator. Even a thin layer between the drive chassis and the backplate creates significant high-frequency impedance, degrading the EMC ground connection. Bare metal contact is required.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Paint is an insulator. The high-frequency return current path between the drive chassis and the backplate is broken by the paint layer, undermining the EMC installation.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — consider what paint does to the electrical connection between the drive and the backplate at high frequency.

pool.stem: A du/dt filter is installed on a drive with a 150 metre motor cable. What is the likely outcome?

options.text: The filter will protect the motor from voltage peaks at the cable end
The filter will have little effect because the long cable itself reduces du/dt at the motor terminals
The filter will increase the risk of voltage reflection in the cable
The filter will prevent common-mode bearing currents from reaching the motor

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. With long motor cables, the cable itself acts as a distributed filter and reduces du/dt at the motor terminals. A du/dt filter at the drive output provides little additional benefit.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Du/dt filters are most useful with short cables. With long cables, the cable's own distributed inductance and capacitance already reduces du/dt significantly at the motor terminals.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — think about how a long motor cable behaves as a transmission line and what that does to the voltage rise-time at the far end.

pool.stem: Which output filter type produces a near-sinusoidal output voltage and eliminates voltage reflections and bearing currents?

options.text: du/dt filter
Common-mode filter
Sine-wave filter
RFI filter

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. The sine-wave filter is an LC low-pass filter whose cut-off frequency is set to eliminate all high-frequency components, producing a near-sinusoidal output voltage and eliminating voltage reflections and bearing currents.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. A du/dt filter only reduces the slew rate while keeping the PWM waveform. A common-mode filter only affects common-mode currents. The sine-wave filter produces a near-sinusoidal output.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — consider which filter type completely removes the high-frequency content from the output voltage waveform.

pool.stem: Why must the PE (protective earth) conductor NOT pass through a common-mode filter core?

options.text: The PE conductor carries DC current that would saturate the core
All ferrite cores are conductive and would short the PE conductor
The PE conductor carries the shield return current, which would cancel the common-mode flux and defeat the filter
The PE conductor must remain floating for the filter to work correctly

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. The PE/shield conductor carries the common-mode return current. Passing it through the core would oppose the common-mode flux produced by the phase conductors, cancelling the filter's effect.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The PE conductor carries the common-mode return current from the cable shield. Passing it through the core would cancel the common-mode flux created by the phase conductors, rendering the filter ineffective.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — think about what current flows in the PE/shield conductor and how it would interact with the flux from the three phase conductors in the core.

pool.stem: A sine-wave filter is specified for an AC drive application. What additional design consideration must be accounted for?

options.text: The filter requires a separate earth connection directly to the supply transformer
The drive output voltage may need to be increased by 7–10% to compensate for the filter's voltage drop
The filter limits the drive to operation below 30 Hz
The filter's output impedance requires a minimum motor cable length of 20 metres

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Sine-wave filters typically cause a 7–10% voltage drop. This may require boosting the drive's output voltage, and pull-out torque also drops faster in the field-weakening range.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Sine-wave filters introduce a 7–10% voltage drop across the filter inductance. The drive output voltage may need to be increased accordingly, and torque performance in field weakening will be affected.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — recall the voltage characteristic of sine-wave filters and what that means for motor operation.

pool.stem: Control cables should be routed in an AC drive installation:

options.text: Parallel to and close to the motor cable to reduce cable tray costs
Segregated from power cables and shielded where analogue signals are used
Inside the drive enclosure, bundled with the DC-link cables
Above the motor cable, separated only by a cable tie

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Control cables must be physically segregated from power cables. When carrying analogue signals, they should also be shielded to prevent inductive and capacitive coupling from the power cables.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Control cables must be separated from power cables and shielded where analogue signals are involved. Running them parallel to a motor cable is exactly the wrong approach.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — consider which cables carry sensitive signals and what happens when they are routed close to high-energy switching conductors.

pool.stem: Common-mode filter cores mounted at the drive output terminals have what disadvantage compared to mounting at the motor terminal box?

options.text: They are too large to fit in the drive cabinet
The capacitive current in a long motor cable can saturate the cores
They cannot reduce bearing currents when mounted at the drive
They require a separate 24 V DC supply to operate

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. When the cores are at the drive terminals, the capacitive displacement current of a long motor cable flows through the cores and can saturate the magnetic material, reducing effectiveness.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The disadvantage of drive-terminal mounting is that a long motor cable's capacitive charge current passes through the cores and can saturate them, degrading performance.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — think about what current flows in the section of cable between the drive and the cores, and how it affects the magnetic material.

screens.label: The Key Numbers
screens.vo: Section four: Bearing Electric Stress. When a motor runs, the bearings are isolated from the stator by a thin film of lubricant. That film is only about 0.5 micrometres thick. A voltage as low as 10 volts across that film is enough to cause a breakdown and an electrical discharge. [PAUSE] Each discharge melts a microscopic pit in the bearing race or ball surface. The accumulation of pits is called pitting. Over time, pits form a regular grooved pattern called fluting — a classic indicator of electrical bearing damage. This progressive erosion of bearing metal is called Electrical Discharge Machining, or EDM.

screens.label: Shaft Voltage Causes
screens.vo: The main cause of EDM in variable speed drive applications is shaft voltage. The root cause is the combination of the inverter's steep du/dt switching and the common-mode voltage it produces. [PAUSE] Capacitive coupling between the stator winding and rotor — via the stator-rotor air gap capacitance — transfers a portion of the common-mode voltage to the rotor shaft. Inductive coupling from circulating stator currents can also contribute. In high-power motors where current exceeds 100 to 200 amperes, asymmetric motor cables are an additional factor — even independent of the drive's switching.

screens.label: Diagnosing Bearing Failures
screens.vo: Not every bearing failure is electrical in origin. Mechanical root causes are in fact the most frequent. Before taking any corrective action, the root cause must be identified. A bearing that has suffered electrical stress typically shows a fluting pattern under visual inspection, with microscopic pitting visible under an electron scanning microscope. A bearing that has suffered mechanical stress — such as incorrect axial loading — shows parallel trenches across the surface. [PAUSE] Here's the key point: when a mechanical problem exists, it will accelerate electrical degradation. Mitigating the electrical stress in that case extends bearing life but does not fix the underlying problem. Mechanical faults must be addressed mechanically first.

screens.label: Mitigation Measures
screens.vo: Mitigation measures fall into two groups: mechanical and electrical. On the mechanical side: verify motor-to-load alignment, check that radial and axial bearing loads are within specification, check vibration levels, and ensure the bearing is correctly lubricated for the operating speed. On the electrical side: provide a low-impedance high-frequency return path for common-mode currents — which means following EMC installation rules, using a shielded cable correctly terminated at both ends, and ensuring the motor chassis has a good high-frequency earth connection. [PAUSE] Additional electrical measures include shaft grounding brushes to directly drain shaft voltage, symmetrical motor cables in high-power applications, and common-mode filter cores. Note that common-mode filters reduce stress but do not replace a correct EMC installation.

screens.label: Installing CM Cores
screens.vo: High-frequency common-mode cores consist of nanocrystalline magnetic rings. All three motor phase conductors must pass through the cores — it is essential to follow the installation instructions exactly. If not all phases pass through, or if the shield or PE conductor is included, the core will saturate or become ineffective. [PAUSE] The cores can be fitted at the drive terminals or inside the motor terminal box. At the drive terminals, the cores also reduce high-frequency noise on the motor cable — but the cable's capacitive current can saturate the cores in long-cable applications. Inside the motor terminal box, saturation risk is eliminated because only the motor current flows through the cores at that point. However, the cable itself will still radiate in that case.

quiz.quiz_vo: Let's test your understanding of bearing electric stress. Four questions from the pool — three correct needed to proceed.
quiz.pass_msg: Excellent — you understand the causes and mitigation of electrical bearing stress. Move on to Section 5.
quiz.fail_msg: Review the section — in particular the EDM mechanism, the diagnostic indicators, and the mitigation strategies — then try again.

pool.stem: What is the approximate thickness of the lubricant film that isolates the bearing races at normal operating speed?

options.text: 0.5 millimetres
5 micrometres
0.5 micrometres
50 nanometres

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. The lubricant film is approximately 0.5 micrometres thick. This extremely thin film means a shaft voltage of only around 10 V can cause an electrical breakdown and discharge.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The lubricant film is approximately 0.5 micrometres (0.5 × 10⁻⁶ metres) thick. Only about 10 V is needed to break it down.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — recall the order of magnitude for the lubricant film thickness and why it makes bearings vulnerable to even low shaft voltages.

pool.stem: The term EDM in the context of motor bearings stands for:

options.text: Electromagnetic Disturbance Mechanism
Electrical Discharge Machining
Electronic Drive Mode
Eddy-current Damping Mechanism

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. EDM stands for Electrical Discharge Machining — the same process used industrially to shape metal, but here occurring destructively inside the bearing.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. EDM stands for Electrical Discharge Machining. The repeated micro-discharges through the lubricant film ablate the metal of the races and balls.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — this abbreviation is borrowed from a manufacturing process that shapes metal using electrical discharges.

pool.stem: A visual inspection of a failed motor bearing reveals a regular grooved pattern across the bearing race surface. What does this most likely indicate?

options.text: The bearing has suffered mechanical overload from incorrect axial loading
The bearing grease has broken down due to excessive temperature
The bearing has suffered electrical discharge damage (EDM), indicated by a fluting pattern
The bearing inner race has spun on the shaft due to incorrect fit

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. A regular grooved fluting pattern is the classic visual indicator of electrical bearing damage caused by repeated EDM discharges over time.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. A fluting pattern — regular grooves across the bearing race — is characteristic of EDM. Mechanical damage typically shows as parallel trenches or pitting from impact loading.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — recall which type of damage produces a characteristic regular groove pattern on the bearing race surface.

pool.stem: What is the primary root cause of increased shaft voltage when a variable speed drive is used, compared to direct-on-line operation?

options.text: The motor runs at variable speed, changing the bearing lubricant film properties
The steep du/dt switching of the inverter combined with the common-mode voltage it produces
The RFI filter inside the drive generates high-frequency voltages on the motor shaft
The DC-link capacitors discharge through the motor winding-to-rotor parasitic capacitance

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. The steep du/dt of the inverter, combined with the common-mode voltage it inherently produces, is capacitively coupled from the stator winding to the rotor — raising shaft voltage above what occurs in direct-on-line operation.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. The key factor is the inverter's steep du/dt switching rate combined with the common-mode voltage at the output. This creates a capacitive coupling path from stator winding to rotor that does not exist to the same degree in direct-on-line operation.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — consider what is different about the motor's applied voltage waveform when a variable speed drive is used compared to a direct sinusoidal supply.

pool.stem: A motor bearing fails repeatedly despite fitting common-mode filter cores. The root cause has not been investigated. What is the most likely explanation?

options.text: The common-mode filter cores are not suitable for this motor type
An unresolved mechanical root cause is continuing to stress the bearing, with electrical stress accelerating the damage
The cores have been fitted at the motor terminal box instead of the drive terminals
The motor cable is too long for common-mode filter cores to be effective

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. If a mechanical root cause is present, electrical mitigation extends bearing life but does not fix the underlying problem. The mechanical fault must be identified and corrected first.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Electrical mitigation can reduce the rate of damage, but if a mechanical root cause exists (misalignment, overloading, incorrect lubrication), the bearing will continue to fail until that is addressed.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — recall the guidance on diagnosing bearing failures and what happens when a mechanical root cause exists alongside electrical stress.

pool.stem: Which measure provides the most direct path to eliminate shaft voltage by draining it away continuously?

options.text: Using a symmetric motor cable
Installing a sine-wave filter
Fitting a shaft grounding brush
Connecting a 100 nF capacitor from shaft to frame

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. A shaft grounding brush makes direct electrical contact with the rotating shaft, providing a continuous low-impedance path to drain shaft voltage to the motor frame before it builds up to the breakdown level.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. A shaft grounding brush makes direct contact with the rotating shaft, providing a low-impedance continuous discharge path that prevents shaft voltage from building up to the lubricant breakdown level.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — consider which measure makes direct electrical contact with the rotating shaft itself.

pool.stem: In a high-power motor installation where motor current exceeds 200 A, which cable type is recommended to reduce shaft voltage from motor asymmetry?

options.text: Standard single-layer braided shielded cable
Symmetrical motor cable
Aluminium armoured cable with steel wire
Unshielded twisted pair

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. In high-power applications, motor cable asymmetry contributes to shaft voltage independently of the drive. Symmetric motor cables (with equally spaced phase conductors) minimise this asymmetric contribution.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. In high-power applications with motor currents over 100–200 A, cable asymmetry itself contributes to shaft voltage. Symmetric motor cables are recommended to minimise this effect.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — recall which cable construction minimises the electromagnetic asymmetry that contributes to shaft voltage in high-power applications.

screens.label: The EMC Directive
screens.vo: Section five: EMC Standards and Compliance. In the European Union, the EMC Directive — currently 2014/30/EU — is a legal requirement. It has two essential requirements: products must not emit electromagnetic interference above defined limits, and they must be immune to a reasonable level of interference from the environment. The directive distinguishes between two categories. Apparatus — finished products sold to end users — must bear the CE mark, backed by a Declaration of Conformity. Fixed installations — permanently installed combinations of equipment, such as a full drive system built into a control panel — are not CE marked, but must be built following good engineering practice and respecting the EMC requirements of the components used.

screens.label: Standards Hierarchy
screens.vo: EMC standards are organised in three layers. At the base, basic standards define general test methods and measurement techniques — for drives, EN 55011 defines the emission limits. Above that, generic standards cover specific environments without targeting a particular product: EN 61000-6-3 and EN 61000-6-1 for residential and commercial environments; EN 61000-6-4 and EN 61000-6-2 for industrial environments. At the top, product standards apply to a specific product family. For AC drives, that standard is EN/IEC 61800-3. [PAUSE] When a product standard exists, it takes precedence over the generic standard for that product. You do not need to demonstrate compliance with both.

screens.label: Drive Categories C1–C4
screens.vo: EN/IEC 61800-3 classifies drives into four categories based on their environment and power level. Category C1 applies to drives on the public low-voltage supply in residential environments — the strictest emission limits. Category C2 applies to drives for restricted use in industrial environments not connected to the public supply — they require a declaration of intent. Category C3 covers drives for industrial environments at higher power levels. Category C4 applies to very high-power systems with complex installations — for these, no emission limits are set, but a site-specific EMC plan is mandatory. [PAUSE] Click each row in the table on screen to see the applicable emission standard and key notes for each category.

quiz.quiz_vo: Final section quiz. Three questions from the pool — two correct needed to proceed to the course summary.
quiz.pass_msg: Well done — you can identify the relevant EMC standards and drive categories. Head to the summary.
quiz.fail_msg: Review the standards and categories section and try again.

pool.stem: Under the European EMC Directive, which of the following requires CE marking?

options.text: A permanently installed AC drive system built into a control panel at a factory
A variable speed drive sold as a finished product to an end user
A fixed installation of drives and switchgear permanently wired into a substation
A custom-built panel assembled for a specific installation project

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Apparatus — finished products made commercially available as a single-function unit to end users — must carry the CE mark. Fixed installations are not CE marked.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. CE marking under the EMC Directive applies to apparatus — commercially sold finished products. Fixed installations (permanently installed systems) are not CE marked but must comply through good engineering practice.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — the directive distinguishes between a product sold commercially and a system permanently installed at a specific location.

pool.stem: Which standard is the product standard for AC drives under the EN/IEC framework?

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. EN/IEC 61800-3 is the dedicated product standard for adjustable-speed electrical power drive systems. It sets both emission and immunity requirements based on the installation environment.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. EN/IEC 61800-3 is the product standard for AC drives. EN 55011 is a basic emission standard, and the EN 61000-6 series are generic standards for different environments.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — recall which standard in the three-level hierarchy applies specifically to the AC drive product family.

pool.stem: An AC drive with rated current over 400 A is installed in a complex industrial system. No fixed emission limits apply. What is the mandatory requirement under EN/IEC 61800-3?

options.text: A Declaration of Conformity to EN 55011 Class A
An EMC plan for the specific installation
Compliance with the generic standard EN 61000-6-4
CE marking of the complete installation

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Category C4 drives (very high power, complex installations) are not subject to fixed emission limits, but an EMC plan specific to the installation is mandatory.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Category C4 applies to drives in very large, complex installations. Rather than fixed emission limits, the requirement is to prepare a site-specific EMC plan.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — recall what the standard requires when a drive falls into Category C4.

pool.stem: A Category C1 drive is more strictly regulated than a Category C3 drive because:

options.text: C1 drives are physically smaller and therefore harder to shield
C1 drives may be connected to the public mains supply in residential environments, where interference affects a wider range of sensitive equipment
C1 applies only to drives above 75 kW, which generate more interference
C1 drives operate at higher switching frequencies

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Category C1 covers drives on the public low-voltage supply where residential, commercial, and light industrial equipment may be connected. Tighter emission limits protect that wider mix of sensitive equipment.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. C1 applies to drives on the public low-voltage supply in residential environments. Because sensitive equipment such as consumer electronics and home appliances share the same supply, much stricter emission limits apply.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — think about what type of environment Category C1 covers and what other equipment may be connected to the same electrical supply.

pool.stem: Generic standard EN 61000-6-4 applies to:

options.text: Immunity requirements in residential environments
Emission requirements in industrial environments
Emission requirements in residential and commercial environments
Immunity requirements in industrial environments

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. EN 61000-6-4 is the generic emission standard for industrial environments. EN 61000-6-2 is the corresponding immunity standard for industrial environments.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. EN 61000-6-4 covers emission in industrial environments. The 6-2 standard covers immunity in industrial environments. The 6-3 and 6-1 standards address emission and immunity in residential/commercial environments.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — recall the pairing of the EN 61000-6 series: which number covers emission vs immunity, and which covers residential vs industrial.

pool.stem: If a product standard exists for a specific product type, what is the relationship to the generic standard?

options.text: Both standards must be demonstrated simultaneously
The product standard takes precedence and the generic standard does not need to be demonstrated
The generic standard takes precedence unless the product standard is more stringent
The product standard applies only to emission; the generic standard still applies to immunity

feedback.correct: ✓ Correct. Product standards take precedence over generic standards for the product type they cover. Compliance with EN/IEC 61800-3 is sufficient; the generic standard need not also be demonstrated.
feedback.wrong: ✗ Not quite. Product standards supersede generic standards for the relevant product category. A manufacturer demonstrating compliance with EN/IEC 61800-3 does not also need to demonstrate compliance with EN 61000-6-4.
feedback.retry: Incorrect — consider the hierarchy of standards: basic → generic → product, and which level takes precedence when it exists.

screens.label: Key Takeaways
screens.vo: Let's review the five key takeaways from this course. First: controlling the common-mode current return path is the central principle of EMC in AC drive systems. Second: cable shield termination must be 360 degrees at both ends — a pigtail or single-end termination defeats the purpose of the shielded cable. Third: choose your output filter based on the application — du/dt for winding stress with short cables, sine-wave for long cables and old motors, common-mode for bearing stress reduction. Fourth: always diagnose bearing failures before treating them — a mechanical root cause must be fixed mechanically. Fifth: know your C-category — it determines which emission limits apply and whether an EMC plan is required.

screens.label: Best Practices
screens.vo: Before you take the final exam, here is an EMC installation checklist. Use shielded motor cable — single-layer braided copper wire is the minimum. Terminate the shield at both ends with 360-degree EMC cable glands. Mount the drive on a bare-metal backplate — remove paint at all mounting points. Segregate control cables from power cables. Provide a potential equalisation conductor of at least 10 square millimetres. Route analogue signal cables in shielded twisted pairs, shielded at both ends. Use digital bus communication rather than analogue signals where possible. And if output filters are needed, specify the correct type and check the voltage drop and cable length implications before you order.

exam.pass_msg: Congratulations — you have passed the EMC for AC Drives assessment. You are now qualified to apply correct EMC practices in AC drive installations and to specify appropriate filters and grounding strategies.
exam.fail_msg: You did not reach the pass mark of 73% (11 out of 15). Review the sections indicated below and retake the assessment. There is no limit on retakes.
section_names: Section 3 — EMC-Correct Installation and Output Filters

questions.stem: Noise is transferred between two circuits via the magnetic field of a current-carrying conductor inducing a voltage in an adjacent conductor. Which coupling mechanism is this?
options: Galvanic coupling
Capacitive coupling
Inductive coupling
Electromagnetic coupling
questions.feedback: Inductive (magnetic) coupling occurs when the magnetic field surrounding a current-carrying conductor induces a voltage in a nearby conductor.

questions.stem: An AC drive produces common-mode current because:
options: The DC-link ripple voltage drives current through the output capacitors
The high du/dt switching voltage acts on parasitic capacitances to ground in the motor cable and motor
The motor back-EMF couples energy back into the drive output stage
The RFI filter generates reactive current at the switching frequency
questions.feedback: The inverter's high du/dt voltage is applied across the parasitic capacitances between the phase conductors and ground (in the cable and motor), producing a common-mode current equal to C × du/dt.

questions.stem: A shielded motor cable shield is connected to the drive chassis with a short wire twisted into a bundle (pigtail). What is the EMC consequence?
options: No consequence — the shield is still connected to ground
The pigtail reduces transfer impedance at high frequency
The pigtail dramatically increases transfer impedance, largely defeating the shielding
The pigtail prevents ground-loop currents from flowing
questions.feedback: A pigtail introduces inductance that increases transfer impedance at the high frequencies present in drive systems, largely defeating the shielding effectiveness of the cable.

questions.stem: For a motor cable in an AC drive installation, the shield must be terminated:
options: At the drive end only, to avoid ground loops
At the motor end only, as the drive chassis provides the return path
At both ends with 360-degree clamp connections
Floating at both ends to prevent saturation of the RFI filter
questions.feedback: Both ends of the motor cable shield must be terminated with a 360-degree clamp connection. Single-end termination significantly reduces shielding effectiveness and can cause interference problems.

questions.stem: A 50 Hz ground current is circulating in the shield of a serial communication cable between a drive and a PLC. What is this phenomenon called?
options: Common-mode saturation
Ground loop
Differential-mode interference
Shield induction
questions.feedback: A ground loop occurs when a voltage potential difference exists between the chassis of two connected pieces of equipment, driving a 50 Hz current through the shield that couples into the signal.

questions.stem: The primary EMC advantage of multi-point grounding over single-point grounding is:
options: It eliminates the need for shielded motor cables
It prevents differential-mode noise from entering the circuit
It minimises the length and therefore the impedance of each ground connection
It ensures only one ground current path exists
questions.feedback: Shorter ground connections have lower impedance, especially at high frequencies. Multi-point grounding keeps each connection short, which is essential for effective high-frequency potential equalisation.

questions.stem: A du/dt filter is most effective in which installation scenario?
options: A 690 V installation with a short motor cable and an older motor with poor insulation
A 400 V installation with a 200 metre motor cable and a modern inverter-rated motor
A regenerative drive with a long cable and high-power motor
Any installation where the drive operates in the field-weakening range
questions.feedback: Du/dt filters are recommended with 690 V supply systems, old motors with poor insulation, and short motor cables. With long cables the cable itself reduces du/dt at the motor, making the filter less effective.

questions.stem: Compared to a du/dt filter, a sine-wave filter:
options: Is cheaper, easier to install, and allows shorter motor cables
Produces a sinusoidal output, eliminates voltage reflections and bearing currents, but introduces a 7–10% voltage drop
Only reduces common-mode current and has no effect on differential-mode voltage
Is only suitable for installations where motor current exceeds 100 A
questions.feedback: A sine-wave filter produces a near-sinusoidal output voltage, eliminating all high-frequency harmonics, voltage reflections, and bearing currents. The trade-offs are higher cost and a 7–10% voltage drop across the filter.

questions.stem: When installing common-mode filter cores on a motor cable, which conductors must pass through the cores?
options: All three phase conductors and the PE conductor
The three phase conductors only — the PE must not pass through
The PE conductor only — the phase currents cancel and have no effect
All conductors including the cable shield
questions.feedback: Only the three phase conductors pass through the cores. The PE (and shield) must not, because its current would cancel the common-mode flux created by the phase conductors, rendering the filter ineffective.

questions.stem: EDM damage in motor bearings is caused by:
options: Excessive bearing load from mechanical misalignment
High-frequency current flowing through the bearing lubricant film, causing repeated electrical discharges
Overheating of the bearing grease due to high switching frequency
Resonance between the motor inductance and the cable capacitance
questions.feedback: EDM damage occurs when shaft voltage exceeds the breakdown voltage of the thin lubricant film (approximately 10 V across 0.5 μm). Each discharge melts a microscopic pit in the bearing metal.

questions.stem: Electron microscope analysis of a failed motor bearing shows parallel trenches with no pitting. Despite correct EMC installation and common-mode filter cores being fitted, the bearing fails repeatedly. The most likely explanation is:
options: The common-mode cores were installed at the motor terminal box instead of the drive
The motor cable is too long for the filter cores to be effective
The bearing failure has a mechanical root cause that must be addressed mechanically
The shaft grounding brush is not making good contact with the shaft
questions.feedback: Parallel trenches without pitting indicate a mechanical root cause (such as axial overloading). Electrical mitigation measures extend bearing life but cannot fix a mechanical fault. The mechanical issue must be resolved first.

questions.stem: In a high-power motor application where motor current exceeds 200 A, which additional factor contributes to shaft voltage independently of the drive's switching?
options: Asymmetric motor cable construction
DC-link voltage ripple at twice the supply frequency
Ground loop currents at 50 Hz
Harmonic content of the motor current
questions.feedback: At high motor currents, asymmetric motor cable construction creates an unbalanced magnetic field that contributes to shaft voltage independently of the drive's common-mode voltage. Symmetric motor cables are recommended to mitigate this.

questions.stem: A variable speed drive rated at 5.5 kW is installed in a domestic building connected directly to the public 230 V / 400 V supply. Which EN/IEC 61800-3 category applies?
options: Category C1
Category C2
Category C3
Category C4
questions.feedback: Category C1 applies to drives connected to the public low-voltage supply in a first environment (residential, commercial, light industrial). It has the strictest emission limits to protect the wide range of sensitive equipment on the same supply.

questions.stem: Under the European EMC Directive, a fixed installation of AC drives built permanently into a factory substation:
options: Must carry the CE mark and a Declaration of Conformity
Is exempt from all EMC requirements
Is not CE marked, but must be built following good engineering practices and the EMC requirements of its components
Must comply with EN 61000-6-4 as a product standard
questions.feedback: Fixed installations are not CE marked under the EMC Directive. Instead, they must be built following good engineering practice and respecting the EMC requirements stated in the documentation of the individual components used.

questions.stem: A very large AC drive installation with motor currents exceeding 400 A falls under EN/IEC 61800-3 Category C4. No fixed emission limits apply. What is the mandatory requirement?
options: Compliance with the generic standard EN 61000-6-4 instead
An installation-specific EMC plan
A Declaration of Conformity to EN 55011 Class A
CE marking of the complete installation
questions.feedback: Category C4 installations have no fixed emission limits. Instead, an installation-specific EMC plan is mandatory. This plan documents the emission sources, their characteristics, and the measures taken to control interference.
